examples.title: Fibonacci using Iteration
examples.description: Compute nth Fibonacci number iteratively avoiding exponential time of naive recursion
examples.explanation: Uses two variables to track previous two Fibonacci numbers, iterating from 2 to n. This avoids the exponential blowup of naive recursive Fibonacci which has recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1).

examples.title: Binary Search Recurrence
examples.description: Divide-and-conquer algorithm with recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], target)
BEGIN
  IF n == 0 THEN
    RETURN -1
  mid = n/2
  IF A[mid] == target THEN
    RETURN mid
  ELSE IF A[mid] > target THEN
    RETURN BinarySearch(A[0..mid-1], target)
  ELSE
    RETURN BinarySearch(A[mid+1..n-1], target)
END

// Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + 1
// Solution: T(n) = O(log n)
examples.explanation: Each recursive call halves the search space. The recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 solves to O(log n) by Master Theorem Case 2 (a=1, b=2, n^log₂(1)=n⁰=1, so f(n)=1 = Θ(1), giving Θ(log n)).

examples.title: Merge Sort Algorithm
examples.description: Classic divide-and-conquer sorting with recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)
examples.code:
ALGORITHM MergeSort(A[0..n-1])
BEGIN
  IF n <= 1 THEN
    RETURN A
  mid = n/2
  left = MergeSort(A[0..mid-1])
  right = MergeSort(A[mid..n-1])
  RETURN Merge(left, right)
END

ALGORITHM Merge(left, right)
BEGIN
  result = []
  WHILE left and right not empty DO
    if left[0] <= right[0] then
      append left[0] to result
      remove first element from left
    else
      append right[0] to result
      remove first element from right
  append remaining elements to result
  RETURN result
END

// Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)
// Master Theorem Case 2: T(n) = Θ(n log n)
examples.explanation: MergeSort divides array in half (2 subproblems of size n/2), then merges in O(n) time. By Master Theorem with a=2, b=2, f(n)=n, we have n^log₂(2)=n, so Case 2 applies giving Θ(n log n).

examples.title: Tower of Hanoi Solution
examples.description: Recursive solution to Tower of Hanoi with recurrence T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1
examples.code:
def tower_of_hanoi(n, source='A', target='C', auxiliary='B'):
    if n == 1:
        print(f"Move disk 1 from {source} to {target}")
        return 1
    
    # Move n-1 disks from source to auxiliary
    moves = tower_of_hanoi(n-1, source, auxiliary, target)
    
    # Move nth disk from source to target
    print(f"Move disk {n} from {source} to {target}")
    moves += 1
    
    # Move n-1 disks from auxiliary to target
    moves += tower_of_hanoi(n-1, auxiliary, target, source)
    
    return moves

# Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1, T(1) = 1
# Solution: T(n) = 2^n - 1 (by iteration or induction)
# Time: O(2^n), Space: O(n) recursion depth
examples.explanation: To move n disks: move n-1 to auxiliary (T(n-1)), move largest disk (1), move n-1 from auxiliary to target (T(n-1)). Total: T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1. Solving by iteration: T(n) = 2^n - 1. Note: This is exponential and impractical for large n.
